title: EFE = Epistemic + Pragmatic

runtime.expected_outcome: Policy posterior prints with the EFE split into epistemic (info-gain) and pragmatic (preference) columns; the two rank policies differently.

symbols.epistemic term: expected information gain from observing o under pi
symbols.p(o): C vector, the agent's log preference over observations

audiences.kid: The agent rates every plan two ways: how much will I learn? and how much will I like the result? EFE is both scores added up.
audiences.real: EFE splits the policy score into 'what do I learn' and 'what do I want'. Seeing the split makes it obvious why exploration can beat exploitation sometimes.
audiences.equation: G(pi) = epistemic + pragmatic. Epistemic ~ -D_KL between posterior and prior predictive; pragmatic ~ -ln p(o) expectation.
audiences.derivation: Factorise q(o,s|pi) = q(s|o,pi) q(o|pi); apply Bayes to swap q(s,o|pi) and q(o|s,pi); the KL term emerges as negative mutual information. See Schwartenbeck et al. (2019) for the modern statement.

orchestrate.objective: Print the per-policy EFE decomposition so the reader can see which term drove the choice.
orchestrate.role: Decomposition tutor tied to Ch 4's epistemic/pragmatic breakdown.
orchestrate.context: forked_paths 7x5 with preference_strength=2.0; a medium pragmatic weight that lets both terms matter.

credits: Parr, Pezzulo & Friston (2022), Active Inference, MIT Press (CC BY-NC-ND), Ch 4.
Schwartenbeck, Passecker, Hauser, FitzGerald, Kronbichler & Friston (2019), Computational mechanisms of curiosity and goal-directed exploration.
